title: Pollen allergy prevention, from before work to after you get home
description: A practical pollen allergy guide covering what to check before you leave, which mask to wear, how to clean up after coming home, and when medication helps most.

sections.heading: 30-second check before you leave
tips: Check pollen and air quality before you head out. The decision is easier before symptoms start.
If the day looks rough, prepare your mask, tissues, eye drops, or medicine before leaving home.
If you have a lunch walk or long outdoor commute ahead, decide early whether you should reduce exposure.
People with repeat severe symptoms usually do better when they plan the day early instead of reacting late.

sections.heading: Masks and outdoor gear
tips: N95 or FFP2 masks are the safest default on higher pollen days because they also help on bad air-quality days.
Surgical masks are better than nothing, but they are weaker once pollen levels climb.
Cloth masks are not a serious pollen strategy.
Fit matters. A strong filter with gaps around the nose is still a weak result.

sections.heading: Indoor Environment Management
tips: Ventilate later in the day when pollen is usually lower, not first thing in the morning.
A HEPA air purifier is most useful in the bedroom because sleep quality drops fast on bad allergy days.
Dry laundry indoors during pollen season. Outdoor drying turns fabric into a pollen collector.
Keep humidity around 40-60%. Dry air makes the nose and throat more reactive.

sections.heading: The 10-minute reset after coming home
tips: Take off outerwear near the door so pollen does not spread through the home.
Wash your hands and face, especially around the eyes and nose.
A saline rinse helps more on heavy days than most people expect.
Showering and washing hair matter because hair holds onto pollen for hours.

sections.heading: When medication helps most
tips: Antihistamines usually work better when taken before symptoms ramp up rather than after you are already miserable.
Nasal steroid sprays are often the strongest option for congestion, but they work on a delay rather than instantly.
Eye drops are worth treating as part of the main toolkit, not an afterthought, if eye symptoms hit you hard.
If the same season hits you every year, planning medication before the season starts is often much better than improvising mid-season.

sections.heading: When long-term treatment is worth discussing
tips: If pollen season keeps wrecking work, sleep, or outdoor plans every year, long-term treatment may be worth discussing.
Immunotherapy takes time, usually years, so it is not a quick fix.
But it is one of the few options that can reduce the underlying allergic response rather than just blunt symptoms.
